title: Hypothesis-Testing
text: estimate Bayes factors single parameter, set parameters, can use bf_table() function. Bayes factors computed via Savage–Dickey density ratio (SDDR), , point null H0:θ=θ0H_0 : \theta = \theta_0 nested H1:θ≠θ0H_1 : \theta \neq \theta_0 shared nuisance parameters, gives BF01=p(θ=θ0∣y)p(θ=θ0), \mathrm{BF}_{01} \;=\; \frac{p(\theta = \theta_0 \mid y)}{p(\theta = \theta_0)}, .e. ratio posterior prior density evaluated null value. bf_table() reports BF10=1/BF01\mathrm{BF}_{10} = 1 / \mathrm{BF}_{01} approximating posterior density θ0=0\theta_0 = 0 MCMC draws. perform hypothesis test effects involving specific variable, can call bf_table() function, specifying variable want look using variable argument. Lets investigate hours sleep individual got previous night (sleep_hour) influence network structure: output now shows 6 rows. five “Fixed Effect” rows show covariate sleep_hour influence node separately. “Fixed Effect (join)” row displays single Bayes factor case tests hypothesis H: difference baseline network structure explained variable sleep_hour can see, BF_{10} = bf_tab[]. Therefore can conlude variable sleep_hour influence baseline level network.
code: bf_tab <- bf_table(fit, variable = "sleep_hour") bf_tab #>                   type  predictor      outcome    BF10 #> 1         Fixed Effect sleep_hour      anxious 0.03386 #> 2         Fixed Effect sleep_hour         calm 0.05187 #> 3         Fixed Effect sleep_hour conventional 0.03760 #> 4         Fixed Effect sleep_hour     critical 0.02951 #> 5         Fixed Effect sleep_hour   dependable 0.03403 #> 6 Fixed Effect (joint) sleep_hour            — 0.00000

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: bvarnet uses -U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), variant Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC), via CmdStan. Unlike simple Metropolis-Hastings, NUTS adapts step-size trajectory length automatically warmup phase, making efficient high-dimensional, correlated posteriors typical multilevel VAR models. Even , every MCMC analysis pass set standard diagnostic checks results interpreted. vignette covers: Fitting model sensible defaults Convergence diagnostics: R-hat Effective Sample Size Trace plots visual inspection Sampler-level warnings: divergences exceeded tree depth Tuning sampler via iter, warmup, adapt_delta, max_treedepth deeper treatment HMC/NUTS theory every diagnostic described , see Stan Reference Manual — MCMC Sampling community Stan Wiki.

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: reuse five-variable ordinal model vignette("bvarnet"). studentlife data used throughout.

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: R-hat (R̂\hat{R}) measures whether independent chains converged distribution comparing within-chain -chain variance. value R̂≈1\hat{R} \approx 1 indicates chains exploring region parameter space. Rule thumb: R̂≤1.01\hat{R} \leq 1.01 modern criterion. Values 1.01 suggest chains converged longer sampling needed. convergence table stored fit$convergence data frame one row per parameter, alongside bulk-ESS tail-ESS. quick summary R-hat distribution across parameters:

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: Even though NUTS chains produce correlated samples, Effective Sample Size (ESS) measures many independent draws correlated sequence worth. bvarnet stores two variants:
code:
ess_bulk <- fit$convergence$ess_bulk ess_tail <- fit$convergence$ess_tail  cat("Bulk ESS — min:", round(min(ess_bulk, na.rm = TRUE)),     "  median:", round(median(ess_bulk, na.rm = TRUE)), "
") #> Bulk ESS — min: 6046   median: 16313 cat("Tail ESS — min:", round(min(ess_tail, na.rm = TRUE)),     "  median:", round(median(ess_tail, na.rm = TRUE)), "
") #> Tail ESS — min: 9176   median: 12179 cat("Parameters with bulk ESS < 400:", sum(ess_bulk < 400, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #> Parameters with bulk ESS < 400: 0

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: trace plot shows sampled value parameter iteration every chain. Healthy chains look like fuzzy caterpillar, mix rapidly chains overlap. Trends, spikes, chains stay separated indicate sampling problems. investigate trace plots, can use mcmc_trace() bayesplot package. Complementary density overlay plots show whether chain-specific posteriors consistent: can sampled parameter, example threshold parameter kappa:
code: phi_pars <- grep("^phi", dimnames(fit$draws)[[3]], value = TRUE)[1:6] # select some lag-coefficient parameters  mcmc_trace(fit$draws, pars = phi_pars) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "Trace plots — first six lag coefficients (phi)") mcmc_dens_overlay(fit$draws, pars = phi_pars) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "Per-chain posterior densities — phi") kappa_pars <- grep("^kappa", dimnames(fit$draws)[[3]], value = TRUE)[1:4]  mcmc_trace(fit$draws, pars = kappa_pars) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "Trace plots — first four cutpoints (kappa)")

previous_headings: Sampler Diagnostics: Divergences and Exceeded Tree Depth
title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: divergence occurs numerical integrator HMC deviates true Hamiltonian trajectory. Even small number divergences can indicate sampler missing regions posterior, leading biased estimates. ignored! Common causes remedies: Funnel-shaped posterior (common hierarchical models) Step size large: increase adapt_delta towards 1 (see ). Model misspecification: revisit prior scale model structure.

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: draw new sample, NUTS simulates trajectory parameter space, taking small steps deciding turn around. max_treedepth argument (default 10) sets hard limit long trajectory can . warning appears, means sampler wanted keep exploring cut — hit limit ready stop. Unlike divergences, mean results biased. slowness warning: sampler extra work produce draw, effective sample size per hour compute time lower . Increasing max_treedepth gives sampler room move, typically resolves warning.

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: bvar() exposes two length arguments: total number draws available inference iter × chains (16 000 defaults ). Increasing iter directly improves ESS proportional computational cost. Increasing warmup can help sampler struggles find typical set (visible long burn-trace plots).

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: adapt_delta (default 0.80) target acceptance rate Stan’s dual-averaging algorithm uses adapting step size warmup. higher target forces smaller step size, reduces discretisation error typically eliminates divergences. Trade-: smaller step sizes mean shorter trajectories per unit time, ESS per second decreases. Increase adapt_delta observe divergences; set near 1 unless needed. Typical values: full details see Stan reference adapt_delta.

title: MCMC-Diagnostics
text: max_treedepth (default 10) caps doubling steps NUTS may take. maximum trajectory length 2max_treedepth2^{\text{max\_treedepth}} leapfrog steps. Increase fit$diagnostics shows non-trivial number iterations hitting tree-depth limit. additional level doubles compute time per affected iteration, increases 1–2 beyond default usually sufficient. full details see Stan reference max_treedepth.

title: Missing-Data
text: Currently, bvarnet handles missing data listwise deletion skipping lag-mechanism. Listwise deletion computational simple solution missing data problem, rely assumption data missing completely random (MCAR). Violating assumption may lead biased parameter estimation. longitudinal settings like Vector Autoregressive models, listwise deletion can introduce irregular gaps observed time series. violates modeling assumption missing time points equal length time gaps. address issue, introduce skipping-lag mechanism. Specifically, lagged effects estimated time difference two observations exceed threshold determined autoregressive order K. , VAR(K) model, lagged relationships included effective time gap corresponds K time steps. words, model excludes estimation temporal parameters across large time gaps, thereby preserving interpretation lagged effects local (short-term) dependencies.

what: Why does skip_lag = FALSE produce fewer observations?
title: Missing-Data
text: skip_lag = FALSE, row whose previous observation exactly one time step away lacks valid lag. autoregressive component computed row, row dropped entirely likelihood. conservative choice: every observation remains genuine lagged value, estimates temporal coefficients (ϕ\phi) unbiased (given model). skip_lag = TRUE (default), rows kept. contribution autoregressive effect set zero, outcome still modeled intercept, fixed effects, (present) random effects. Oly temporal component “switched ” row.

title: Missing-Data
text: two approaches trade sample size potential bias autoregressive estimates: skip_lag = FALSE — Every row valid lag, ϕ\phi estimates reflect true short-term dynamics. However, datasets many irregular gaps (present one), large share observations discarded, reducing statistical power parameters. skip_lag = TRUE — observations contribute estimation intercept, fixed effects, residual variance. zero-filled lags treated “temporal information” rather “lag zero”, rows gaps gently pull ϕ\phi toward zero. datasets many gaps can lead slight weakening autoregressive estimates. Currently, sophisticated missing data handling (e.g. imputation) implemented users .

title: bvarnet
text: First need specify prior distributions want use parameters. can use set_priors() function. running ordinal model, without covariates, specify priors temporal structure (denoted Φ\Phi matrix) category threshold parameters (κ\kappa) specific adjacent category ordinal model. Currently, three prior families implemented: normal, student-t chauchy. standart deviations random effects priors automatically scaled half-priors. distribution, location scale parameters can specified. student-t distribution, degrees freedom can also specified.

title: Compute Savege-Dickey Bayes factors — bf_table
text: Computes Savage-Dickey density ratio Bayes factors (requested set ) parameter model. default, applicable parameters tested returned tidy data frame. type argument controls parameter groups included; variable argument can used filter effects involving specific variables. log_BF10 argument allows including natural log Bayes factor output, round controls numeric rounding results.

title: Compute Savege-Dickey Bayes factors — bf_table
code: bf_table(   object,   type = "all",   lag = 1L,   null_value = 0,   variable = NULL,   log_BF10 = FALSE,   round = 5L )

title: Compute Savege-Dickey Bayes factors — bf_table
text: object bvarnet object returned bvar(). type Character vector specifying parameter groups test. Use "" (default) include applicable groups automatically. Available options: "ar" Autoregressive effects (self-loops). Per-cell BFs lag specified lag, plus joint BF. "cl" Cross-lagged effects. structure "ar". "intercepts" Intercept parameters. Skipped automatically ordinal outcomes. "fe" Non-intercept fixed effects (covariates). "lag_fe" Joint BFs lag \(\times\) covariate interaction terms. available model fitted fe_interactions containing lag terms. "temporal" Joint BF entire temporal structure (AR + CL parameters across lags). lag \(\times\) covariate interactions present, additional omnibus rows included. lag Integer; lag block use (default 1). Applies "ar" "cl" types. null_value Numeric scalar; null hypothesis value (default 0). variable Character vector NULL (default).  One variable names.  set, effects involving variables included. log_BF10 Logical; TRUE, additional log_BF10 column (natural log BF10) appended output. Default FALSE. round Integer NULL; number decimal places round numeric output columns.  Default 5.  Set NULL disable rounding.

title: Compute Savege-Dickey Bayes factors — bf_table
text: data frame columns: type, predictor, outcome, BF10 (optionally log_BF10).

what: Fit a Bayesian multilevel VAR network model — bvar
title: Fit a Bayesian multilevel VAR network model — bvar
text: bvar function estimates posterior distribution specified Bayesian (Multilevel) Vector Autoregression.

title: Fit a Bayesian multilevel VAR network model — bvar
code: bvar(   id_col,   time_col,   y_cols,   x_cols = NULL,   center_x = FALSE,   fe_interactions = NULL,   re_interactions = NULL,   re_cols = NULL,   re_temporal = FALSE,   K = 1,   na_action = c("listwise"),   skip_lag = TRUE,   data,   family = c("bernoulli", "ordinal", "gaussian"),   priors = set_priors(),   iter = 4000,   warmup = 1000,   chains = 4,   cores = 1,   seed = NULL,   adapt_delta = NULL,   max_treedepth = NULL,   save_data = FALSE )

title: Fit a Bayesian multilevel VAR network model — bvar
text: id_col Character. Name subject/group identifier column. time_col Character. Name time column. y_cols Character vector. Names outcome columns. x_cols Character vector NULL. Names covariate columns. center_x Logical. Grand-mean centre covariates fitting? Default FALSE. fe_interactions List NULL. Fixed-effect interaction terms add design matrix. element character vector column names interact, c("lag", "x") interact lag columns covariate. re_interactions List NULL. Random-effect interaction terms. re_cols Character vector. Columns X /"Intercept" include random slopes. re_temporal Logical. Include random slopes lag predictors? Default FALSE. K Integer. AR order. Default 1. na_action Character. Missing-data strategy; currently "listwise". skip_lag Logical. TRUE (default), rows irregular time gaps lag set zero rather dropped. data Data frame long format. family Character scalar vector. Observation model per node. scalar recycled y_cols. vector length length(y_cols) (named positional) specifies per-node families. Valid values: "bernoulli", "ordinal", "gaussian". priors bvarnet_priors object set_priors(). Defaults set_priors() (package defaults). iter Integer. Number post-warmup iterations per chain. Default 4000. warmup Integer. Number warmup iterations per chain. Default 1000. chains Integer. Number MCMC chains. Default 4. cores Integer. Number chains run parallel. Default 1. seed Integer NULL. RNG seed. adapt_delta Numeric (0, 1). Target average proposal acceptance probability warmup adaptation. Higher values (e.g., 0.95–0.99) reduce divergences cost slower sampling. Default NULL (CmdStan default 0.8). max_treedepth Integer. Maximum depth NUTS binary tree. Increasing allows sampler take leapfrog steps per iteration, can help difficult posteriors (e.g., funnels hierarchical logistic models) increases computation. Default NULL (CmdStan default 10). save_data Logical. TRUE, store preprocessed (sorted, listwise-deleted) estimation data data_used slot returned object reproducibility downstream analyses. Default FALSE.

title: Fit a Bayesian multilevel VAR network model — bvar
text: bvarnet object (named list) slots: draws, convergence, diagnostics, timing, metadata, return_codes, family, standata, priors. save_data = TRUE, also includes data_used (cleaned estimation data frame).

title: Simulate data from a multilevel VAR model — sim_var
text: N Integer. Number subjects (groups). T_obs Integer. Number time points per subject. p Integer. Number outcome nodes. K Integer. AR order (default 1). family Character. One "bernoulli", "ordinal", "gaussian". alpha Numeric vector length p. Population intercepts (logit scale bernoulli, identity gaussian). ordinal, absorbed kappa left NULL. Generated NULL. gamma Matrix q x p. Population covariate effects. Generated NULL q > 0. Phi Matrix (p*K) x p. Population lag coefficients. Generated NULL. sigma Numeric vector length p. Residual SD per node (gaussian ). Generated NULL. kappa List p ordered vectors, length C-1. Cutpoints per node (ordinal ). Generated NULL. q Integer. Number covariates (default 0). x_gen Function f(N, T_obs) returning N x T_obs x q array covariates. NULL, default generation used. sd_alpha Numeric. SD random intercepts (scalar p-vector). Default 0.5. Set 0 simulate fixed-effects-model -person variation intercepts. sd_phi Numeric. SD random lag coefficients (scalar matrix). Default 0.2. sd_gamma Numeric NULL. SD random covariate slopes. NULL means random slopes covariates. re_temporal Logical. Include random slopes lag predictors? Default FALSE. C Integer. Number ordinal categories (ordinal , default 5). burnin Integer. Number time points discard warmup recording data (default 500). VAR process simulated burnin + T_obs time points per subject, first burnin discarded. allows process reach stationary distribution data collection begins. seed Integer NULL. RNG seed.
